key_insight: Personalizing a soft robotic exoglove with hand scans and finite-element modeling improves actuator-to-finger alignment and targeted joint mobility for dexterous rehabilitation.
engineering_relevance: The work shows how anatomy-aware design and pHRI simulation can guide soft actuator optimization for safer, more effective hand-assist devices.
method: Topological hand scans used to tailor glove geometry and fabricate silicone mold-cast exogloves.
FEA evaluated actuator bending and contact forces against a personalized biomechanical finger model.
Pneumatic experiments tested static and dynamic pressure control for MCP and PIP joint flexion.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports successful scan-based fit, simulation of contact forces and joint mobilization, and pressure-controlled actuation that accurately flexed the user’s MCP and PIP joints.
watch_item: Results suggest some non-ideal proximal phalanx compression, so clinical use will need careful tuning of pressure, alignment, and strain-limiting design.
paper_title: Soft Robotic Exogloves for Dexterous Mobility -- Towards Personalized Rehabilitation
